title: HTTP & HTTPS
summary: HTTP is the shared set of rules browsers and servers use to talk.
A protocol just means an agreed way of doing something.
HTTPS is the same as HTTP but with security added.
The S stands for Secure, and the padlock in the address bar means it is on.

sections.text: Every time a browser and a server talk, they follow a shared set of rules so they understand each other. That set of rules has a name, and there is a safer version that protects your information.

sections.title: What is HTTP?
sections.text: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol. A protocol is just an agreed way of doing something. HTTP is the agreement browsers and servers use to send requests and responses, so both sides always know what the other means.

sections.title: What is HTTPS?
sections.text: HTTPS is HTTP with a layer of security added. The S stands for Secure. It scrambles the information traveling between your browser and the server, so nobody in the middle can read it.

media.alt: A browser address bar showing a padlock and https

sections.title: A Postcard vs a Sealed Letter
sections.text: Plain HTTP is like sending a postcard. Anyone who handles it along the way can read it. HTTPS is like sealing your message in a locked envelope. Only the person it is meant for can open it.

media.alt: A postcard being read by others, then a sealed locked envelope

sections.title: What HTTPS Does for You
items: It hides your data while it travels across the internet.
It checks that the server is really who it claims to be.
It stops others from secretly changing the data on the way.
It shows a padlock in the address bar so you know it is on.

sections.text: Browsers now warn you when a site uses plain HTTP, because so much of the web has moved to HTTPS that the unsafe version stands out.

terms.definition: The rules browsers and servers use to send requests and responses.
HTTP with security added, so the data is scrambled and safe.
An agreed way of doing something that both sides follow.
Scrambling data so only the right person can read it.
The small lock icon in the address bar that shows HTTPS is on.

questions.text: What does the S in HTTPS stand for?
questions.explanation: The S stands for Secure. HTTPS adds a layer of security on top of plain HTTP.

questions.text: What does the word protocol mean here?
options: An agreed way of doing something
questions.explanation: A protocol is just an agreed set of rules that both sides follow so they understand each other.

questions.text: What does HTTPS do that plain HTTP does not?
options: It scrambles the data so others cannot read it
questions.explanation: HTTPS encrypts the data as it travels, so anyone in the middle cannot read it.
